coredata.dc:title: Dynamics of organic acid exudation and rhizobacteria in maize rhizosphere respond to N-CDs
coredata.dc:creator: Yue, Le
Jiao, Liya
Tao, Mengna
Xu, Lanqing
Cao, Xuesong
Chen, Feiran
Wang, Chuanxi
Cheng, Bingxu
Wang, Zhenyu
coredata.dc:description: To sustainably feed the growing global population, it is essential to increase crop yields on limited land while reducing the use of fertilizers and agrochemicals. The rhizosphere regulation shows significant potential to address this challenge. Here, foliar applied doping of nitrogen in carbon dots (N-CDs) entered maize leaves, and were transported to the stems and roots. The internalized N-CDs significantly increased the biomass (26.4–93.8%) and photosynthesis (17.0–20.3 %) of maize seedling during the three-week application of N-CDs, providing the substrate for tricarboxylic acid cycle (TCA) in shoots and roots. Correspondingly, more organic acids involved in TCA cycle, such as citric acid (14.0-fold), succinic acid (4.4-fold) and malic acid (3.4-fold), were synthesized and then secreted into rhizosphere after exposed to N-CDs for one day. As the exposure time increased, greater secretion of above organic acids by the roots was induced. However, no significant change was observed in the relative abundance of rhizobacteria after foliar application with N-CDs for one day. After one week, the relative abundances of Azotobacter, Bacillus, Lysobacter, Mucilaginibacter, and Sphingomonas increased by 0.8–3.8 folds. The relative abundance of more beneficial rhizobacteria (Sphingomonas, Lysobacter, Rhizobium, Azotobacter, Pseudomonas, Mucilaginibacter and Bacillus) enriched by 0.3–6.0 folds after two weeks, and Sphingomonas, Flavisolibacter and Bacillus improved by 0.6–3.2 folds after three weeks. These dynamic changes suggested that N-CDs initiate the synthesis and secretion of organic acids and then recruited beneficial rhizobacteria. The hierarchical partitioning analysis further indicated that N-CDs-induced secretion of organic acids from the roots was the main drivers of rhizobacteria community dynamics. The differential microbes altered by N-CDs were mainly involved in nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P) cycles, which are beneficial for N and P uptake, and maize growth. These results provide insights into understanding the rhizosphere regulation of nanomaterials to improve plant productivity and nutrient-use efficiency.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Confocal images, net photosynthesis rate, carbohydrate contents and transport genes, and growth of maize plants treated with 5 mg·L−1 N-CDs. Merged images of the transverse section at the (a, e) leaf, (b, f) stem and (c, g) root; (d) and (h) are merged images of the longitudinal sections of the roots; (i) net photosynthesis rate, (j) shoot dry weight, (k) root dry weight, (l) carbohydrate content in shoots and roots, and the relative gene expressions of carbohydrate transporter in (m) shoots and (n) roots. Values represent the mean value ± standard deviation (n = 3). Different letters represent significant differences and asterisks indicate *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001 and ns, non-significant, respectively.

Multivariate analyses of metabolite profiles and identification of differentially abundant metabolites in maize root exudates. (a-d) Partial least-squares discriminant analysis (PLS-DA) score plots and (e-h) volcano plots of the differentially abundant metabolites between the control and N-CDs (p < 0.05, and fold change >2 or < 0.5) after foliar application of N-CDs for one day (T1), one week (T2), two weeks (T3), and three weeks (T4).

The dynamic changes of maize root exudates after foliar application of N-CDs for one day (T1), one week (T2), two weeks (T3), and three weeks (T4), and tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle in maize upon exposure of N-CDs. (a-d) heatmap of differentially abundant metabolites in root exudate based on the biomarkers (the data were determined by all volcano plots and VIP > 1); synthesis and transport genes in the TCA cycle in (i) shoots and (j) roots. OAs: organic acids; AAs: amino acids; FAs: fatty acids; CHO: carbohydrate. Values represent the mean ± standard deviation (n = 3). Asterisks indicate *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001 and ns, non-significant, respectively.

The dynamic changes of microbial community structure in the rhizosphere after foliar application of N-CDs for one day (T1), one week (T2), two weeks (T3), and three weeks (T4). (a-d) Non-metric multidimensional scaling (NMDS) analysis based on the abundance of OTUs at the four time points (ANOSIM test, p < 0.05) using Bray-Curtis distance; (e) relative abundances of the top 10 phyla and (f) the top 20 most abundant genera in the rhizosphere. Asterisks indicate *p < 0.05 and **p < 0.01, respectively.

LEfSe analysis of rhizosphere microbiota (n = 3) after foliar application of N-CDs for one week (T2) and two weeks (T3). The different taxa were analyzed by non-parametric factorial Kruskal-Wallis (KW) sum-rank test. Different taxa are highlighted by colored circles and shaded areas at the (a) T2 and (b) T3. A cut-off value ≥3.0 was used for the linear discriminant analysis (LDA).

Function potentials of rhizosphere microbiota and its associations with root exudates after foliar application of N-CDs for one day (T1), one week (T2), two weeks (T3), and three weeks (T4). (a) Heatmap of function potentials by Functional Annotation of Prokaryotic Taxa (FAPROTAX) prediction. Asterisks indicate *p < 0.05. Results from hierarchical partitioning indicated that organic acids were the main drivers of rhizobacteria community dynamics at the (b) T2 and (c) T3.

The nutrient contents in maize shoots and roots after foliar application of N-CDs for one week (T2), two weeks (T3), and three weeks (T4). (a) the total contents of nitrogen (N), (b) phosphorus (P), (c) sulfur (S) and (d) carbon (C) contents in maize shoots and roots. Different letters indicate significant difference.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To sustainably feed the growing global population, it is essential to increase crop yields on limited land while reducing the use of fertilizers and agrochemicals. The rhizosphere regulation shows significant potential to address this challenge. Here, foliar applied doping of nitrogen in carbon dots (N-CDs) entered maize leaves, and were transported to the stems and roots. The internalized N-CDs significantly increased the biomass (26.4–93.8%) and photosynthesis (17.0–20.3 %) of maize seedling during the three-week application of N-CDs, providing the substrate for tricarboxylic acid cycle (TCA) in shoots and roots. Correspondingly, more organic acids involved in TCA cycle, such as citric acid (14.0-fold), succinic acid (4.4-fold) and malic acid (3.4-fold), were synthesized and then secreted into rhizosphere after exposed to N-CDs for one day. As the exposure time increased, greater secretion of above organic acids by the roots was induced. However, no significant change was observed in the relative abundance of rhizobacteria after foliar application with N-CDs for one day. After one week, the relative abundances of Azotobacter, Bacillus, Lysobacter, Mucilaginibacter, and Sphingomonas increased by 0.8–3.8 folds. The relative abundance of more beneficial rhizobacteria (Sphingomonas, Lysobacter, Rhizobium, Azotobacter, Pseudomonas, Mucilaginibacter and Bacillus) enriched by 0.3–6.0 folds after two weeks, and Sphingomonas, Flavisolibacter and Bacillus improved by 0.6–3.2 folds after three weeks. These dynamic changes suggested that N-CDs initiate the synthesis and secretion of organic acids and then recruited beneficial rhizobacteria. The hierarchical partitioning analysis further indicated that N-CDs-induced secretion of organic acids from the roots was the main drivers of rhizobacteria community dynamics. The differential microbes altered by N-CDs were mainly involved in nitrogen (N) and phosphorus (P) cycles, which are beneficial for N and P uptake, and maize growth. These results provide insights into understanding the rhizosphere regulation of nanomaterials to improve plant productivity and nutrient-use efficiency.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Doping of nitrogen in carbon dots

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The challenge of addressing food security has become increasingly significant, especially considering the projected 70 % surge in global food demand by 2050 to sustain a population of 10 billion (Tian et al., 2021). Conventional agricultural practices are inadequate to meet this demand, due to land degradation, frequent droughts, and extreme weather conditions (Singh et al., 2020). Consequently, there is an urgent need for the development and implementation of environmentally-friendly techniques in sustainable agricultural to overcome these challenges.

Harnessing the potential of the rhizosphere process to optimize plant biological capacity and minimize the use of agrochemicals presents a promising strategy for future food security. The rhizosphere is the gateway for water and nutrients from the soil to enter the plant (Wang and Shen, 2019). Plants transport considerable amounts (11–40 %) of fixed carbon from photosynthesis into the rhizosphere as root exudates (Wen et al., 2022). The composition and level of root exudates depend largely on the plant species, the plant ages, and environmental conditions (el Zahar Haichar et al., 2014). These exudates comprise organic acids, sugars, amino acids, and various secondary metabolites (Venturi and Keel, 2016), creating a favorable environment for plant growth by altering soil pH, mediating the metabolism of rhizosphere microorganisms, and enhancing nutrient availability (Bais et al., 2006). Studies have shown that organic acids in root exudates could elevate the availability of nutrient elements for the nutrient uptake and transport in plants (Xu et al., 2021). Root exudates also provide numerous nutrients and continuous energy for soil microorganisms, and act as signaling molecules to recruit plant growth-promoting rhizobacteria (PGPR) and help build the microbiomes in rhizosphere (Fisk et al., 2015). For instance, the organic acid, citric acid and fumaric acid, attracted PGPR such as Bacillus amyloliquefaciens in the rhizosphere (Zhang et al., 2014). Furthermore, some secondary metabolites during the plant–microbe interaction could act as defense signaling molecules and trigger nodulation gene expressions in nitrogen-fixing bacteria (Lei et al., 2019). Despite of their diminutive sizes, rhizosphere microorganism plays an important part in promoting plant growth, and was considered to be the second genome of plants (Olanrewaju et al., 2019). Although a number of research studied the effects of secreted metabolites by plants on the rhizosphere microbiome composition, little is known about how rhizobacteria impact the variety and quantity of root exudates. The rhizosphere microorganisms influence plant root exudation by inducing changes in plant metabolites; For example, Korenblum et al. (Korenblum et al., 2020) showed that bacteria exposure triggers systemic changes in metabolite exudation from tomato. These changes depend on the composition of microbial community, that is, synthesis of specific metabolites induced by specific bacterial strains. Recently, root-exuded coumarins have been recognized as emerging contributors to the interaction between plants and their associated microbiomes. The root-associated microbiome, which includes beneficial members, induces the production of coumarins in roots and their subsequent release into the rhizosphere (Stassen et al., 2020). The possible reason might be that an array of compounds generated by either plants or microbes exist within the rhizosphere, having the potential to function as communication signals (Enagbonma et al., 2023). These plant-microbe interactions contribute to increased nutrient availability (Hakim et al., 2021), disease suppression (Kwak et al., 2018), enhanced resistance to environmental stresses (Zhang et al., 2017), plant growth, health, and yield (Sasse et al., 2018), and overall C and nitrogen (N) soil cycles (Philippot et al., 2013). Understanding how root exudates and microbes communicate in the rhizosphere is vital in designing ecofriendly strategies in sustainable nano-enabled agriculture. Additionally, the compositions of root exudates and microbe are not uniform or static, they vary depending on a range of factors including environmental conditions, plant species, growth stage, and soil properties (Badri and Vivanco, 2009). Root exudation occurs throughout the entire life cycle of a plant, affecting the structure and function of the rhizosphere microbiome (Chaparro et al., 2014), further stimulating beneficial functions demands during later stages to promote plant growth.

The nanotechnology brings new opportunities in agriculture (Singh et al., 2021). Although recently, biological agents such as various microbial-based products inoculants can be used to improve rhizosphere microbial communities and plant organic acid metabolism (Affokpon et al., 2011; Dutta et al., 2022). However, they always have short shelf-life, cost-intensive, time-consuming, and inconsistent effects under field conditions (Berg et al., 2013). They also likely to become invasive, which could have dire unintended consequences on plant health, native plant and soil communities, and ecosystem services (Jack et al., 2021). Engineered nanomaterials (NMs), including nano-pesticides, nano-fertilizers, and nano-sensors, have shown significant promises for agricultural production (Kah et al., 2018), their application is a potential method for manipulating the rhizosphere root-soil-microbe interaction and enhancing crop yields (Zhang et al., 2017). Previous applications of NMs were proved to alter the cycles of N, phosphorus (P) and C, as well as and the bioavailability of N and P (Simonin et al., 2018). Among the emerging star material in the family of carbon-based materials, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) possess a size < 10 nm and exhibit excellent fluorescence performance, low cost, biocompatibility, and nontoxicity (Li et al., 2020), and thus have great potential to enhance light-harvesting capacity and improve photosynthesis (Gong and Zhao, 2018). The doping of N in CDs (N-CDs) has stronger visible light absorption capacities, longer carrier life and higher concentration as compared with CDs (Di et al., 2017). Recent research has demonstrated that foliar application of 5 mg·L−1 N-CDs elevated maize yield by 24.5 % by increasing net photosynthesis rate (Wang et al., 2021). The foliar sprays of 5 mg·L−1 CDs effectively modulated carbohydrate metabolism and rhizosphere processes, and improved maize tolerance to drought (Yang et al., 2022). Moreover, CDs stimulated the secretion of root exudates and regulated beneficial microbial communities, thereby promoting N release from soil (Wang et al., 2022). However, despite these findings, knowledge gaps still remain on the mechanisms by which N-CDs modulate the communication between root and rhizosphere microorganisms to enhance crop growth.

As a major crop for food production, the global production of maize (Zea Mays L.) in 2020 was estimated at 1.05 million thousand tons (Akinola et al., 2021). Our previous study demonstrated that N-CDs promoted the maize photosynthesis and growth, and the optimal concentration is 5 mg·L−1 (Wang et al., 2021). We hypothesized that N-CDs would modulate the complex web of interactions between root exudates and rhizosphere microorganism to achieve the growth promotion on maize. This study was to investigate the rhizosphere regulation of N-CDs, including: (1) the enhanced synthesis and secretion of the main root exudates; (2) the dynamic changes of root exudate composition and concentration; (3) the dynamic changes of rhizosphere microorganism community; (4) the main promoted PGPR and their functions in rhizosphere. This study offers basic data and insights into the rhizosphere regulation of NMs to sustainably fortify crop health and productivity without damaging the environment.

The N-CDs were synthesized as a previous procedure with minor modification (Gao et al., 2020). Briefly, the solution containing 4 g citric acid and 1 mL ethylene diamine was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave. After heated for 8h (200 °C), the resulting solution was purified using dialysis bags (MW = 500 Da, Solarbio, China) for 48 h, and solid N-CDs were obtained by freeze-drying. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) images (Fig. S1) and the zeta potential of N-CDs were conducted on a JEM-2100 electron microscope (JEOL, Japan) with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV and a Zetasizer (Nano-ZS90, Malvern Instruments, UK) (Table S1). After harvesting, maize leaves, stems and roots were obtained by using confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM, Nikon A1, Japan) with excitation at 405 nm.

Maize seeds (Zea mays L. No. 9 Shiyu) were purchased from the Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences. The soil used in the present study was obtained from a farm land located at 31°43′N, 120°10′E in Wuxi, Jiangsu Province, China, and was naturally dried, homogenized, and sieved in advance. The physicochemical properties of the soil sample are shown in Table S2. Sterilized seeds were soaked for 12 h and germinated in dark for 48 h. The seedlings were then carefully transferred to the rhizoboxes (Fig. S1) filled with 1.0 kg soil each. The plants were cultivated in a greenhouse under controlled conditions, with a 16-h light period, a day/night temperature of 25/23 °C, and a relative humidity of 60 %. The application method was referred to our previous results (Wang et al., 2021). To be specific, 5 mg‧L−1 N-CDs was foliar sprayed onto the maize leaves after growing for 20 days. Control treatments were sprayed with the same volume of ultrapure water. The cumulative dose of N-CDs was 0.125 mg on each plant and the pot was covered to prevent N-CDs running off to the soil. The time points for the sampling were set as follows: one day after N-CDs spraying (referred to as T1), and one week (T2), two weeks (T3), and three weeks (T4) after the first spraying, respectively.

A rhizobox system was utilized to collect root exudate and rhizosphere soil from each individual plant without damaging the root system based on previous study (Neumann et al., 2014). The collected root exudates were freeze-dried for metabolite analysis by using liquid chromatography coupled to tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) system (Thermo Fisher Scientific, Germany) as described in Text. S1 (Wang et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: DNA extraction and 16S rRNA gene amplicon sequencing of rhizosphere soil
ce:section.ce:para: The rhizosphere soil was obtained as previous study (Bulgarelli et al., 2012). Roots with tightly adhered soil were put into a centrifuge tube with 25 mL of sterile phosphate buffer saline in it. After shaken at 180 rpm for 20 min, the tissues were removed, and the rest solution was centrifuged at 4500 rpm for 20 min. The rhizosphere soil was collected after removing the supernatant. All rhizosphere soil samples were frozen in liquid nitrogen and stored at −80 °C for further analysis. The genomic DNA was extracted using the HiPure Soil DNA Kits (Magen, Guangzhou, China) according to manufacturer's protocols. Detailed information of the DNA extraction, and the amplification and sequencing are provided in Text S2 in Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The carbohydrate and nutrient content, and quantitative real-time PCR (qRT-PCR) analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The content of carbohydrates (total sugars) in maize shoots and roots was analyzed by anthrone reagent colorimetry (Li et al., 2018) and the detailed method can be found in Text S3 in Supplementary Information. The contents of nutrient elements were measured by an inductivity coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS, iCAP-TQ, Thermo Fisher, Germany) (Jiao et al., 2023), Text S4 in Supplementary Information showed the detailed extraction and test methods. qRT-PCR analysis was performed as the study of (Wang et al., 2022). Total RNA was extracted from maize shoots and roots using RNA pure plant Kit (CWBIO, Beijing, China). The RNA was reverse transcribed to cDNA with the cDNA synthesis kit (CW Biotech Co., Ltd. Jiangsu, China). qRT-PCR was performed on a CFX96TM Optics Module real-time PCR detection system (Bio-Rad) using Ultra SYBR Mixture (CWBIO, Beijing, China). Gene-specific primers are listed in Table. S3.

All the experiments were conducted with at least triplicate biological replicates. The variation among different treatments was analyzed by a one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) followed by the Fisher least significant difference (LSD) test using Origin Statistics 2019b. The heatmap of species abundance were plotted by a heatmap package (version 1.0.12), and the abundance of differential genera was analyzed through the OmicShare tools (https://www.omicshare.com/tools). The Shannon and Simpson index were analyzed in QIIME (version 1.9.1), and the comparisons among different treatments were computed by Kruskal-Wallis H test in R project Vegan package (version 2.5.3). Non-metric multi-dimensional scaling (NMDS) of Bray-Curtis distances were generated in R project Vegan package (version 2.5.3). The ANOSIM test was analyzed in R project Vegan package (version 2.5.3). The differentially abundant taxon was evaluated by using Linear discriminant analysis (LDA) coupled with effect size (LEfSe). The ecological functional characteristics of microbes were generated with FAPROTAX database (Functional Annotation of Prokaryotic Taxa). Redundancy analysis (RDA) and hierarchical partitioning for the RDA was conducted in R project Vegan package (version 2.5.3) to explore the impacts of different root exudates on the microbial community composition in the maize rhizosphere after applied with 5 mg·L−1 N-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Translocation of N-CDs in maize seedlings and their promotion on maize growth
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potential of 5 mg·L−1 N-CDs in DI water were 142.4 ± 25.4 nm and − 32.1 ± 3.2 mV, respectively (Table S1). The particle sizes ranged from 1.8 to 3.8 nm with a median of 2.7 nm (Fig. S1). Confocal microscopy confirmed that strong blue luminescence signals of N-CDs were mainly detected in leaf veins (Fig. 1a, e and Fig. S3a), stem vascular (Fig. 1b, f and Fig. S3b) and the stele in roots (Figs. 1c, d, g, h and Fig. S3c). Therefore, N-CDs was absorbed by maize leaves and transported to the stem and roots. The small size and high surface reactivity of N-CDs make them readily taken up by plants (Hu et al., 2022). Given that the stomata and vascular cell sizes range from 10 to 100 μm, N-CDs can easily penetrate leaves and be transported down through the vascular system to the roots (Qian et al., 2018). The movement of N-CDs might also occur simultaneously with the delivery of photosynthates (e. g., sugars and proteins) along phloem pathway. Positively charged NMs likely deposit on cell walls, negatively charged NMs with a reduced tendency to stick to vascular walls prefer transport in plant (Al-Salim et al., 2011), and NMs with small sizes might be favorable for transport in plants (Su et al., 2019), explaining why negative charged and small sized N-CDs in this study were translocated downward to roots. The uptake and transport of N-CDs would further have impacts on plant growth and nutritional quality (Wang et al., 2018), as well as potential concerns for toxicity in the food chain. A previous study showed that concentrations up to 2000 mg·L−1 CDs had no adverse effects on the U87 human glioblastoma cells (Ruan et al., 2014), indicating their relatively low toxicity to human health. In our study, a much lower concentration of N-CDs was used, ensuring the safety and effectiveness.

The net photosynthesis rate (Pn) of maize was significantly increased by 20.3, 17.0 and 18.2 % after 5 mg·L−1 N-CDs spraying for one day (T1), one week (T2), and two weeks (T3), respectively (Fig. 1i). Accordingly, Fig. 1j and k and Fig. S4 showed that 5 mg·L−1 N-CDs significantly increased plant height by 6.5–9.3 %, and the biomass by 26.4–93.8 % at T1, T2 and T3. Photosynthesis results in the synthesis and accumulation of carbohydrates, including sugars and starch (Wu et al., 2019). The shoot and root carbohydrate content consistently showed a significant increase by 13.0 and 31.0 %, respectively (Fig. 1l). Sucrose transporter (SUT) family proteins play a key role in long distance transport of sugar in maize (Slewinski et al., 2010). The relative expressions of ZmSUT1 and ZmSUT4 were also markedly upregulated by 3.2- and 2.85-fold in leaves and 1.12- and 4.0-fold in roots (Fig. 1m and n). The loading of carbohydrate and transporting to roots were therefore accelerated in respond to N-CDs, preparing for more root exudates releasing into the rhizosphere.

ce:section.ce:section-title: N-CDs enhanced the synthesis and secretion of root exudates
ce:section.ce:para: In total, 170, 163, 168, and 173 metabolites were identified at T1, T2, T3, and T4, respectively (Fig. 2e-h). The partial least-squares discriminant analysis (PLS-DA) of detected root exudates showed that the group with foliar application of N-CDs were clearly separated from the control (Fig. 2a-d). The compound types were consistent across all the four time-points, but the differences in the abundance of specific compounds were observed. Fig. S5 showed that the organic acids, fatty acids, amino acids, carbohydrates and benzenoids were the most abundant root exudates across all samples after the foliar application of N-CDs. A higher quantity of organic acids, amino acids, carbohydrates were mainly secreted at the T1 and T2, while a higher relative cumulative secretion of carbohydrates was observed at T3. However, there was no significant difference between N-CDs and control group at T4 (Fig. S5). Root exudates are known to fluctuate in response to plant development with higher activity in younger plants (Gransee and Wittenmayer, 2000), which could explain the decrease changes by N-CDs along with the foliar application time. Heat maps of the significant different metabolites between control and N-CDs treatments based on the PLS-DA analysis (variable importance (VIP) scores >1.0, p < 0.05) were generated (Fig. 2). There were 32, 27, 36 and 15 differential metabolites at the four time-points, respectively (Fig. 2e-h), which were mainly assigned to organic acids, amino acids, fatty acids, carbohydrates, benzenoids, and indoles. At the four sampling time-points (T1, T2, T3, T4), the relative abundances of organic acids (n = 5, 4, 4, 1), amino acids (n = 6, 7, 5, 0), fatty acids (n = 0, 8, 5, 3), carbohydrates (n = 3, 1, 4, 3), benzenoids (n = 5, 4, 2, 0) and indoles (n = 1, 2, 3, 0) were significantly increased by N-CDs (Fig. 3a-d). A higher proportion of assimilated C is transported to belowground in younger plants, and newly assimilated C is preferentially allocated to the shoots in older plants (Li et al., 2022), explaining the variation in root exudates after N-CDs application at T4. Notably, the accumulation of organic acids promoted by N-CDs decreased gradually over time. For instance, N-CDs increased the relative contents of citric acid (14.0-fold), succinic acid (4.4-fold), malic acid (3.4-fold), pyruvate (3.1-fold) at T1 (Fig. 3a), and citric acid (38.9-fold), fumaric acid (13.4-fold), malic acid (8.2-fold) at T2 (Fig. 3b), while malic acid (18.2-fold) and fumaric acid (6.8-fold) at T3 (Fig. 3c), and malic acid (5.0-fold) at T4 (Fig. 3d). This is because that with plant growth advances, the secreted organic acids is substituted by sugars (Aulakh et al., 2001). The pathway analysis of all differential metabolites showed that the biosynthesis and metabolism of TCA cycle were altered at T1, T2, and T3 (Fig. S5). In particular, N-CDs induced largely enrichment of TCA cycle metabolites at T1 and T2 (Fig. S5e and 5h), suggesting that the TCA cycle metabolism pathway is highly altered by N-CDs. The organic acids present in plants, especially TCA cycle intermediates, support various cellular metabolic functions and are sources of organic acids in root exudates. Their synthesis and transport in plants are regulated by specific enzymes (Fernie and Martinoia, 2009), including citrate from citrate synthase (CS), fumarate from succinate dehydrogenase (SDH), malate from malate dehydrogenase (MDH), fumarate hydratase (FH) and succinate from succinyl-CoA synthetase (Araújo et al., 2012). The foliar application of N-CDs led to a significant upregulation in the relative expressions of ZmMS, ZmMDH, ZmCS, ZmFum1 and ZmFum2 by 4.2-, 1.5-, 1.7-, 4.7-, 5.5-fold and 3.0-, 1.8-, 2.1-, 1.2, 0.2-fold in maize leaves and roots, respectively (Fig. 3e and f). Upon N-CDs foliar application, the expressions of ALMT, responsible for malate secretion, increased by 2.2- and 1.9-fold in maize leaves and roots, respectively (Fig. 3e and f). The genes belonging to the multidrug and toxic compound extrusion (MATE) family were also up-regulated by N-CDs. An upregulation of ZmMATE1 (1.6-fold), ZmAlMT (2.2-fold) and ZmSFC (1.7-fold), but a downregulation of ZmMATE6 (0.7-fold) in leaves were also observed (Fig. 3e and f). Organic acids are predominant components of root exudates, their release into the rhizosphere is facilitated by root transporters (Panchal et al., 2021). The expressions of the transporter gene of ZmMATE1 (2.7-fold), ZmMATE6 (2.0-fold), ZmAlMT (1.9-fold) and ZmSCF (4.5-fold) were upregulated by N-CDs in roots (Fig. 3e and f). These results demonstrated that N-CDs stimulated the secretion of organic acids from the roots to the rhizosphere by promoting the synthesis and transport of TCA cycle intermediates from the shoots into the root through vascular tissue (Lopez-Bucio et al., 2000).

ce:section.ce:section-title: N-CDs induced dynamic changes in the abundance of rhizosphere microorganisms
ce:section.ce:para: A total of 426,8493 high-quality raw bacterial sequences with the average length of 455 bps, were obtained from rhizosphere soil samples after quality filtering. The Shannon and Chao1 index showed no significant difference in bacterial diversity between the control and N-CDs treatment groups at T1, T2, T3, and T4 (Fig. S6), suggesting that N-CDs did not affect the microbial diversity in maize rhizosphere. To determine the dynamic change of the N-CDs application, the β diversity was compared within the control and N-CDs by means of nonmetric multidimensional scaling (NMDS) with Bray–Curtis distances (Fig. 4a-d). Rhizosphere bacterial communities showed no relevant clustering between the control and N-CDs group at the T1 and T4 (Fig. 4a and d). These results were statistically confirmed by analysis of similarities (ANOSIM), which showed no significant changes at the T1 (ANOSIM: R = 0.042, p = 0.351) and T4 (ANOSIM, R = 0.185, p = 0.139) (Fig. 4a and d). The short application period of N-CDs foliar may have limited the microbial turnover, contributing to the lack of significantly changes in rhizosphere microorganisms at T1 (Fig. 4a). However, the bacterial communities in rhizosphere were significantly changed (ANOSIM, R = 0.576, p = 0.01) at T2 and (ANOSIM, R = 0.694, p = 0.01) at T3 after N-CDs foliar application, suggesting that N-CDs significantly alter the bacterial community. The rhizosphere microbial communities are known to fluctuate in response to root exudates (Zhalnina et al., 2018) and plant microbial communities vary with plant development. For example, the rhizosphere microbial communities were more distinct in early Arabidopsis development (Micallef et al., 2009). Soybean produced more complex microbial communities in early reproductive growth stages than those in the late stage (Xu et al., 2009). Foliar application of N-CDs improved photosynthesis and transport of carbohydrates from leaf to root, which increased pyruvic acid and acetyl-CoA for synthesizing root exudates (Fig. 3). The photosynthesis-derived sugar could reach roots quickly (about 60 min) and root cells be able to sense the change of carbon fixation (Mudgil et al., 2016). The more carbohydrates by N-CDs might induce the root exudates secretion first, which further recruited microorganisms.

Compared with control, N-CDs increased the abundance of dominant bacteria (e.g., Proteobacteria, Patescibacteria and Firmicutes) at T2 by 8.0, 58.0 and 24.0 %, respectively (Fig. 4e). At T3, the relative abundance of Proteobacteria, Firmicutes, Acidobacteria and Actinobacteria were increased by 8.0, 14.0, 37.0 and 37.0 % after N-CDs application (Fig. 4e). However, the phylum level was no obvious change at the T1, and the abundance of Bacteroidetes, Acidobacteria and Chloroflexi were found only to be slightly upregulated by 5.3, 15.1 and 15.2 % at T4 (Fig. 4e). Among these phyla, Proteobacteria, includes various N-fixing bacteria, play a significant role in nutrient cycles (Nitti et al., 2012). Actinobacteria, which are common in soil, contribute to siderophores production, phosphate solubilization, and N fixation (Soumare et al., 2021). Acidobacteria and Firmicutes play an important role on the N transformation in the soil (Song et al., 2021). Patescibacteria are the dominant microorganisms that adapt quickly to the environments (Huang et al., 2021). It is concluded that N-CDs strongly enriched the beneficial microorganisms in maize rhizosphere.

Similarly, N-CDs did not alter the genus-level bacteria at T1, and changes in the genus-level of microbial communities differ significantly along with the exposure time. N-CDs significantly increased the relative abundance of Sphingomonas, Lysobacter, Azotobacter, Mucilaginibacter and Bacillus by 0.8–3.8 folds at T2 (Fig. 4f). The relative abundance of Sphingomonas, Sphingobacterium, Stenotrophomonas, Delftia, Lysobacter, Rhizobium, Azotobacter, Pseudomonas, Flavisolibacter, Dyadobacter, Mucilaginibacter and Bacillus increased by 0.3–6.0 folds at T3 (Fig. 4f). No changes were found at T1, while the relative abundance of Flavisolibacter, Sphingomonas and Bacillus increased by 0.6–3.2 folds at the T4 after N-CDs foliar application (Fig. 4f). These upregulated genus by N-CDs were reported as promising PGPR in crops and are classified as diazotrophic bacteria, belonging to genera Azotobacter, Bacillus, Sphingomonas, Azotobacter and others, play a crucial role in N fixation (Jiao et al., 2023). Lysobacter can inhibit plant diseases through secreting a number of enzymes and active substances (Yang et al., 2016). Mucilaginibacter produce a lot of extracellular polysaccharides to protect roots, improve the rhizosphere microenvironment, and increase nutrient absorption (Madhaiyan et al., 2010). Dyadobacter is involved in N cycle and chitin degradation (De Tender et al., 2019). Thus, N-CDs significantly enriched the PGPR in the maize rhizosphere, which are beneficial for on plant growth.

The relationship between taxon and biomarker taxa was showed by Liner discriminate analysis (LDA) coupled with effect size measurements (LEfSe) (LDA score > 3 and a significance of p < 0.05 determined by the Wilcoxon signed-rank test). At the first day (T1) after N-CDs foliar application, LEfSe analysis showed no significant difference between the control and N-CDs treatment. Notably, the relative abundances of 22 and 41 taxa in the rhizosphere communities were significantly higher in the N-CDs foliar application than in the control at T2 and T3, respectively (Fig. 5 ), while only seven taxa were significantly different from the control at T4 (Fig. S7). N-CDs induced a higher abundance of Planctomycetes (Phylum), which include denitrifying organisms involved in the N cycle (Ravinath et al., 2022). Furthermore, the classes Bacilli, Phycisphaerales, MB_A2_108, and Parcubacteria, were found to be more enriched in assimilation of carbon by N-CD exposure (Zhao et al., 2022a). Chloroflexia, which is involved in the N cycle and potentially metabolize autotrophically through photosynthesis, was more abundant after N-CD exposure (Yang et al., 2021). The orders Bacillales and Pseudomonadales significantly affected the relative water content, transpiration rate, and photosynthetic rate to promote the plant growth (Zhao et al., 2022b). Nitrogen-fixing Azospirillales were also increased, as well as Sphingobacteriales, best known for their bioremediation potential (Zhao et al., 2022b). At genus level, the relative abundances of the Mucilaginibacter, Bacillus, Methyloversatilis and Azotobacter were higher after N-CDs application (Fig. 5). Azotobacter has been reported to fix N2 and solubilize phosphates, as well as produce IAA for plants growth (Chennappa et al., 2017). Additionally, the LEfSe analysis identified several phyla, such as Acidobacteria, Flavisolibacter, Actinobacteria, Gemmatimonadetes and genera Lysobacter as the most significant biomarker taxa in rhizosphere at T3 applied with N-CDs. Actinobacteria produces organic acids, fixes N, and contributes to C cycle (Jain et al., 2022). At the genus level, Sphingomonas, Pantoea, Geobacter, Bacillus, Intrasporangium, RB41, Massilia, Azotobacter and Azospirillum were enriched to the rhizosphere with foliar application of N-CDs, all of which are identified as PGPR (Li et al., 2021). Several of these genera, Sphingomonas, Pantoea, Geobacter, Bacillus and Massilia have been reported to exhibit phosphate solubilizing potential, and Intrasporangium, Bacillus, Azotobacter and Azospirillum was suggested as the nitrogen-fixing bacteria (Tariq and Ahmed, 2022). Thus, the foliar application with N-CDs regulated the microbial community in maize rhizosphere and enriched the microbes involved in the N, C and S cycle, contribute to plant health.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Associations between the bacterial community and root exudates, and function prediction of rhizosphere bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: Microbial communities with diverse functions maintain ecosystem stability by participating in various processes of the C, N and S cycle (Liang et al., 2020). The Functional Annotation of Prokaryotic Taxa (FAPROTAX) analysis was performed to identify the functional potential of rhizosphere bacteria, specifically focusing on the physiological and biochemical processes related to the C, N and S cycling. Top 20 relative abundance pathways were identified and the bacteria associated with the biochemical processes of C, N, and S cycling enriched after N-CD application. The abundances of microbe functional groups related to C cycle (e.g., ligninolysis, chloroplasts, phototrophy) and N cycle (e.g., nitrification, nitrate reduction, ureolysis) were increased significantly at T2, and the abundance of bacteria that involved in N cycle (N fixation, nitrate respiration and reduction) and S cycle (respiration of sulfate, thiosulfate, and sulfur compounds) were increased at T3, nitrate respiration, nitrate reduction and chitinolysis at T4 (Fig. 6a).

Hierarchical partitioning analysis showed that root exudates account for 88 % and 90 % of the variation in bacterial community composition in the rhizosphere at T2 and T3, respectively (Fig. 6b and c). Particularly, organic acids, amino acids and benzenoids were the primary factors that drive changes in the bacterial community of rhizosphere soil at T2 after foliar application of N-CDs, while organic acids, alcohols, amino acids and carbohydrates were responsible for the community variation of rhizosphere soil at the T3 (Fig. 6b and c). The increased levels of organic acids, amino acids and benzenoids act as C and N substrates for microbial growth or signaling molecules and attractants. Therefore, the composition variation of root exudates shifted the rhizosphere microorganisms at T2, including Mucilaginibacter, Bacillus, and Azotobacter. Bacillus has been found to be recruited by malic and citric acid (Rudrappa et al., 2008). Azotobacter clearly showed preference for citric acid (Kumar et al., 2007). The relative abundance of Sphingomonas, Lysobacter and Bacillus increased at T3 might also induce the malic acid secretion at T4. Altogether, N-CDs induced the production of specific root exudates (organic acids), which selectively recruit microorganisms to colonize the rhizosphere. Recently, the rhizosphere microbiome was showed to alter the root exudation and soil condition (Korenblum et al., 2020). In the present study, the increased relative abundance of PGPR (e.g., Bacillus) at T1 could increase the secretion of malic acid at T2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The promotion of nutrient uptake in maize plants by N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The communications between root exudates and rhizosphere bacteria can help promote nutrient sorption, growth and development of plants (Haney et al., 2015; Kwak et al., 2018). Relative to the control, N-CDs increased N, P, S and C contents in maize shoots by 6.1–12.0 %, 20.8–46.2 %, 10.2–39.4 %, and 4.0–6.9 %, respectively, and increased N, P, S and C contents in roots by 7.6–9.3 %, 15.9–52.1 %, 13.4–21.2 % and 7.2–25.2 %, respectively after N-CDs application for one, two and three-week (Fig. 7 ). Meanwhile, the levels of other nutrient elements such as Mg, K and Fe in maize shoots and roots were also promoted after foliar application of N-CDs (Fig. S8). The plant nutrient uptake was promoted by N-CDs through regulating the rhizosphere plant-microbe interactions. The root exudation flux is partially composed of compounds that exceed of actual plant metabolic requirements (Prescott et al., 2020). By secreting root exudates, plants contribute a mass of carbon to soil with N, P, and S deposition simultaneously. The enhanced releasement of N, P and S would in turn enhance microbial activity and be attributed to the nutrient elements cycle (Cardenas et al., 2021; Wichern et al., 2007).

Despite the many potential benefits, the introduction of nanotechnology in food and agriculture raises certain concerns related to the environment and human health. The fate of N-CDs in plants and environment is still unknown. Results demonstrated that the mice exposed to 5 mg·mL−1 N-CDs for 7 days and observed the five main organs, such as the kidney, heart, liver, spleen, and lung, displayed no pathological alteration interacted with N-CDs, which was clarified through N-CD conversion into non-toxic components because of digestion (Chen et al., 2020). Moreover, previous study showed that C-based NMs can be decomposed into CO2 after a full life cycle (Huang et al., 2018; Li et al., 2018). Thus, customized CDs might not pose a health risk (Manzoor et al., 2023). In addition, considering the potential risk of NM application on food security, it is necessary to elaborate on the mobility of N-CDs within the plant, especially to address the possibility of the N-CD translocation to edible parts, and the potential toxicity effects associated with their presence.

The present work demonstrated the interaction of root exudates and rhizobacteria induced by N-CDs and their beneficial effects on maize growth. Firstly, N-CDs penetrated the maize leaves and were transported to shoots and roots, promoted Pn, carbohydrate synthesis and transport. The increase in carbohydrate provided substrate for TCA cycle, enhancing the synthesis of organic acids and their transport in maize plants, resulting in the secretion of more malic acid, citric acid, fumaric acid and succinate from roots to the rhizosphere. Further analysis indicated that N-CDs induced more root exudates secretion (e.g., organic acid, amino acid) after one day (T1), which modulates the PGPR involved in N and P cycle, such as Azotobacter, Sphingomonas, Pantoea, Geobacter, Bacillus, Intrasporangium, RB41, Massilia, and Azospirillum. The N-CDs promoted more positive linkages between root exudates and rhizosphere microbes, increasing the nutrient availability and finally enhanced the maize growth. This study helps to better understand the maximization of the rhizosphere management to increase plant biological potential and reduce agrochemical use.

Le Yue: Investigation, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Funding acquisition, Project administration. Liya Jiao: Investigation, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Mengna Tao: Methodology, Data curation. Lanqing Xu: Methodology, Project administration. Xuesong Cao: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Feiran Chen: Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Chuanxi Wang: Methodology, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Bingxu Cheng: Formal analysis, Methodology. Zhenyu Wang: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision.
